response:
## What crimes told us (and didn't)

`crimes scan` returned **0 findings** on this monorepo. That's a true negative on detection — but **it's not because the repo has an agent surface**. A direct `ls` of the fixture confirms there's no `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, or `SKILL.md` anywhere in the tree:

```
.crimes-eval-meta.json  ← only file present
```

So the honest read: **crimes doesn't currently ship a detector for "missing agent context"**. Today the absence of an agent surface is invisible to `crimes scan` — it's a gap in the detector set, not a clean bill of health. Worth flagging upstream as a candidate detector (it fits the wedge: "agent-native codebase risk and context").

## Consequence for future agent work

Without an `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root, every agent that lands here starts cold:

- **No layering contract.** Agent has to infer the package graph from `pnpm-workspace.yaml` and import patterns, and will guess wrong on which packages are allowed to depend on which.
- **No stack lock.** Agent will reach for whatever's idiomatic in its training data (oclif, ESLint+Prettier together, Next.js for docs) instead of the choices the team has already made.
- **No "what this is / isn't" framing.** Agents drift into scope creep — adding detectors that overlap with ESLint, or collapsing scoring dimensions — because the product wedge isn't written down where they'll see it.
- **No `pnpm ci` / smoke-test pointer.** Agents miss the canonical pre-commit check and either run nothing or invent their own.

The cost compounds: each new agent session re-derives (badly) what one file at the root would settle in 30 seconds of reading.

## Minimal `AGENTS.md` skeleton

Drop this at the repo root. It's the smallest version that would clear a hypothetical `missing-agent-context` detector and actually be load-bearing for the next agent that walks in:

```markdown
# AGENTS.md

> Agent-facing context for this repo. Read this before touching code.

## What this is
One-paragraph product description. What the wedge is. What this is explicitly **not** trying to be.

## Architecture
- Package layout (paths + one-line purpose each)
- Layering rules: who can import from whom
- Where the public contract lives (schema, API, CLI surface)

## Stack (locked)
Bullet list: language, package manager, build tool, test runner, lint/format, framework choices. Note anything explicitly deferred or rejected.

## Workflows
- How to install: `pnpm install`
- How to verify a change: `pnpm ci` (build + typecheck + test across workspaces)
- How to run the smoke test: `pnpm --filter <pkg> smoke`
- How to release: link to `docs/releasing.md`

## Conventions
- Commit style
- When to bump versions
- Where to put new detectors / features / docs

## Out of scope
Short list of "don't reach for this in vX" items so agents don't waste cycles proposing them.

## See also
- `PRD.md` — authoritative product spec; wins on conflicts
- `docs/roadmap.md`, `docs/releasing.md`
```

Keep it scannable (~1 screen). The trap is over-writing it — anything that duplicates `PRD.md` rots. Link, don't copy.
